Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## FOURIER NEURAL OPERATOR FOR PARAMETRIC PARTIAL DIFFERENTIAL EQUATIONS

**Zongyi Li** zongyili@caltech.edu

**Nikola Kovachki** nkovachki@caltech.edu

**Kamyar Azizzadenesheli** kamyar@purdue.edu
**Burigede Liu Kaushik Bhattacharya Andrew Stuart** bgl@caltech.edu bhatta@caltech.edu astuart@caltech.edu

**Anima Anandkumar** anima@caltech.edu

## ABSTRACT

The classical development of neural networks has primarily focused on learning mappings between finite-dimensional Euclidean spaces. Recently, this has been generalized to neural operators that learn mappings between function spaces. For partial differential equations (PDEs), neural operators directly learn the mapping from any functional parametric dependence to the solution. Thus, they learn an entire family of PDEs, in contrast to classical methods which solve one instance of the equation. In this work, we formulate a new neural operator by parameterizing the integral kernel directly in Fourier space, allowing for an expressive and efficient architecture. We perform experiments on Burgers’ equation, Darcy flow, and Navier-Stokes equation. The Fourier neural operator is the first ML-based method to successfully model turbulent flows with zero-shot super-resolution. It is up to three orders of magnitude faster compared to traditional PDE solvers. Additionally, it achieves superior accuracy compared to previous learning-based solvers

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Many problems in science and engineering involve solving complex partial differential equation (PDE) systems repeatedly for different values of some parameters. Examples arise in molecular dynamics, micro-mechanics, and turbulent flows. Often such systems require fine discretization in order to capture the phenomenon being modeled. As a consequence, traditional numerical solvers are slow and sometimes inefficient. For example, when designing materials such as airfoils, one needs to solve the associated inverse problem where thousands of evaluations of the forward model are needed. A fast method can make such problems feasible.

**Conventional solvers vs. Data-driven methods.** Traditional solvers such as finite element methods (FEM) and finite difference methods (FDM) solve the equation by discretizing the space. Therefore, they impose a trade-off on the resolution: coarse grids are fast but less accurate; fine grids are accurate but slow. Complex PDE systems, as described above, usually require a very fine discretization, and therefore very challenging and time-consuming for traditional solvers. On the other hand, data-driven methods can directly learn the trajectory of the family of equations from the data. As a result, the learning-based method can be orders of magnitude faster than the conventional solvers.

Machine learning methods may hold the key to revolutionizing scientific disciplines by providing fast solvers that approximate or enhance traditional ones (Raissi et al., 2019; Jiang et al., 2020; Greenfeld et al., 2019; Kochkov et al., 2021). However, classical neural networks map between finite-dimensional spaces and can therefore only learn solutions tied to a specific discretization. This is often a limitation for practical applications and therefore the development of mesh-invariant neural networks is required. We first outline two mainstream neural network-based approaches for PDEs – the finite-dimensional operators and Neural-FEM.
**Finite-dimensional operators.** These approaches parameterize the solution operator as a deep convolutional neural network between finite-dimensional Euclidean spaces Guo et al. (2016); Zhu

& Zabaras (2018); Adler & Oktem (2017); Bhatnagar et al. (2019); Khoo et al. (2017). Such approaches are, by definition, mesh-dependent and will need modifications and tuning for different resolutions and discretizations in order to achieve consistent error (if at all possible). Furthermore, these approaches are limited to the discretization size and geometry of the training data and hence, it is not possible to query solutions at new points in the domain. In contrast, we show, for our method, both invariance of the error to grid resolution, and the ability to transfer the solution between meshes.

**Neural-FEM.** The second approach directly parameterizes the solution function as a neural network (E & Yu, 2018; Raissi et al., 2019; Bar & Sochen, 2019; Smith et al., 2020; Pan & Duraisamy, 2020). This approach is designed to model one specific instance of the PDE, not the solution operator. It is mesh-independent and accurate, but for any given new instance of the functional parameter/coefficient, it requires training a new neural network. The approach closely resembles classical methods such as finite elements, replacing the linear span of a finite set of local basis functions with the space of neural networks. The Neural-FEM approach suffers from the same computational issue as classical methods: the optimization problem needs to be solved for every new instance. Furthermore, the approach is limited to a setting in which the underlying PDE is known.

**Neural Operators.** Recently, a new line of work proposed learning mesh-free, infinitedimensional operators with neural networks (Lu et al., 2019; Bhattacharya et al., 2020; Nelsen & Stuart, 2020; Li et al., 2020b;a; Patel et al., 2021). The neural operator remedies the mesh-dependent nature of the finite-dimensional operator methods discussed above by producing a single set of network parameters that may be used with different discretizations. It has the ability to transfer solutions between meshes. Furthermore, the neural operator needs to be trained only once. Obtaining a solution for a new instance of the parameter requires only a forward pass of the network, alleviating the major computational issues incurred in Neural-FEM methods. Lastly, the neural operator requires no knowledge of the underlying PDE, only data. Thus far, neural operators have not yielded efficient numerical algorithms that can parallel the success of convolutional or recurrent neural networks in the finite-dimensional setting due to the cost of evaluating integral operators. Through the fast Fourier transform, our work alleviates this issue.

**Fourier Transform.** The Fourier transform is frequently used in spectral methods for solving differential equations, since differentiation is equivalent to multiplication in the Fourier domain. Fourier transforms have also played an important role in the development of deep learning. In theory, they appear in the proof of the universal approximation theorem (Hornik et al., 1989) and, empirically, they have been used to speed up convolutional neural networks (Mathieu et al., 2013). Neural network architectures involving the Fourier transform or the use of sinusoidal activation functions have also been proposed and studied (Bengio et al., 2007; Mingo et al., 2004; Sitzmann et al., 2020). Recently, some spectral methods for PDEs have been extended to neural networks (Fan et al., 2019a;b; Kashinath et al., 2020). We build on these works by proposing a neural operator architecture defined directly in Fourier space with quasi-linear time complexity and state-of-the-art approximation capabilities.

2

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

**Our Contributions.** We introduce the Fourier neural operator, a novel deep learning architecture able to learn mappings between infinite-dimensional spaces of functions; the integral operator is restricted to a convolution, and instantiated through a linear transformation in the Fourier domain.

- The Fourier neural operator is the first work that learns the resolution-invariant solution operator for the family of Navier-Stokes equation in the turbulent regime, where previous graph-based neural operators do not converge.

- By construction, the method shares the same learned network parameters irrespective of the discretization used on the input and output spaces. It can do zero-shot super-resolution: trained on a lower resolution directly evaluated on a higher resolution, as shown in Figure 1.

- The proposed method consistently outperforms all existing deep learning methods even when fixing the resolution to be 64 _×_ 64. It achieves error rates that are 30% lower on Burgers’ Equation, 60% lower on Darcy Flow, and 30% lower on Navier Stokes (turbulent regime with Reynolds number 10000). When learning the mapping for the entire time series, the method achieves _<_ 1% error with Reynolds number 1000 and 8% error with Reynolds number 10000.

- On a 256 _×_ 256 grid, the Fourier neural operator has an inference time of only 0 _._ 005s compared to the 2 _._ 2 _s_ of the pseudo-spectral method used to solve Navier-Stokes. Despite its tremendous speed advantage, the method does not suffer from accuracy degradation when used in downstream applications such as solving the Bayesian inverse problem, as shown in Figure 6.

We observed that the proposed framework can approximate complex operators raising in PDEs that are highly non-linear, with high frequency modes and slow energy decay. The power of neural operators comes from combining linear, global integral operators (via the Fourier transform) and nonlinear, local activation functions. Similar to the way standard neural networks approximate highly non-linear functions by combining linear multiplications with non-linear activations, the proposed neural operators can approximate highly non-linear operators.

We have experimented with linear as well as neural network parameterizations of _Rφ_ . We find that the linear parameterization has a similar performance to the previously described direct parameterization, while neural networks have worse performance. This is likely due to the discrete structure of the space Z _[d]_ . Our experiments in this work focus on the direct parameterization presented above.

**Invariance to discretization.** The Fourier layers are discretization-invariant because they can learn from and evaluate functions which are discretized in an arbitrary way. Since parameters are learned directly in Fourier space, resolving the functions in physical space simply amounts to projecting on the basis _e_[2] _[πi][⟨][x,k][⟩]_ which are well-defined everywhere on R _[d]_ . This allows us to achieve zero-shot super-resolution as shown in Section 5.4. Furthermore, our architecture has a consistent error at any resolution of the inputs and outputs. On the other hand, notice that, in Figure 3, the standard CNN methods we compare against have an error that grows with the resolution.

**Quasi-linear complexity.** The weight tensor _R_ contains _k_ max _< n_ modes, so the inner multiplication has complexity _O_ ( _k_ max). Therefore, the majority of the computational cost lies in computing the Fourier transform _F_ ( _vt_ ) and its inverse. General Fourier transforms have complexity _O_ ( _n_[2] ), however, since we truncate the series the complexity is in fact _O_ ( _nk_ max), while the FFT has complexity _O_ ( _n_ log _n_ ). Generally, we have found using FFTs to be very efficient. However a uniform discretization is required.

## 5 NUMERICAL EXPERIMENTS

In this section, we compare the proposed Fourier neural operator with multiple finite-dimensional architectures as well as operator-based approximation methods on the 1-d Burgers’ equation, the 2-d Darcy Flow problem, and 2-d Navier-Stokes equation. The data generation processes are discussed in Appendices A.3.1, A.3.2, and A.3.3 respectively. We do not compare against traditional solvers (FEM/FDM) or neural-FEM type methods since our goal is to produce an efficient operator approximation that can be used for downstream applications. We demonstrate one such application to the Bayesian inverse problem in Section 5.5.

We construct our Fourier neural operator by stacking four Fourier integral operator layers as specified in (2) and (4) with the ReLU activation as well as batch normalization. Unless otherwise specified, we use _N_ = 1000 training instances and 200 testing instances. We use Adam optimizer to train for 500 epochs with an initial learning rate of 0 _._ 001 that is halved every 100 epochs. We set _k_ max _,j_ = 16 _, dv_ = 64 for the 1-d problem and _k_ max _,j_ = 12 _, dv_ = 32 for the 2-d problems. Lower resolution data are downsampled from higher resolution. All the computation is carried on a single Nvidia V100 GPU with 16GB memory.

**Remark on Resolution.** Traditional PDE solvers such as FEM and FDM approximate a single function and therefore their error to the continuum decreases as the resolution is increased. On the other hand, operator approximation is independent of the ways its data is discretized as long as all relevant information is resolved. Resolution-invariant operators have consistent error rates among different resolutions as shown in Figure 3. Further, resolution-invariant operators can do zero-shot super-resolution, as shown in Section 5.4.

Increasing it degrades their performance while FNO achieves the same errors.

**2D and 3D Convolutions.** FNO-2D, U-Net, TF-Net, and ResNet all do 2D-convolution in the spatial domain and recurrently propagate in the time domain (2D+RNN). The operator maps the solution at the previous 10 time steps to the next time step (2D functions to 2D functions). On the other hand, FNO-3D performs convolution in space-time. It maps the initial time steps directly to the full trajectory (3D functions to 3D functions). The 2D+RNN structure can propagate the solution to any arbitrary time _T_ in increments of a fixed interval length ∆ _t_ , while the Conv3D structure is fixed to the interval [0 _, T_ ] but can transfer the solution to an arbitrary time-discretization. We find the 3-d method to be more expressive and easier to train compared to its RNN-structured counterpart.

8

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## 5.4 ZERO-SHOT SUPER-RESOLUTION.

The neural operator is mesh-invariant, so it can be trained on a lower resolution and evaluated at a higher resolution, without seeing any higher resolution data (zero-shot super-resolution). Figure 1 shows an example where we train the FNO-3D model on 64 _×_ 64 _×_ 20 resolution data in the setting above with ( _ν_ = 1e _−_ 4 _, N_ = 10000) and transfer to 256 _×_ 256 _×_ 80 resolution, demonstrating super-resolution in space-time. Fourier neural operator is the only model among the benchmarks (FNO-2D, U-Net, TF-Net, and ResNet) that can do zero-shot super-resolution. And surprisingly, it can do super-resolution not only in the spatial domain but also in the temporal domain.

## 5.5 BAYESIAN INVERSE PROBLEM

In this experiment, we use a function space Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) method (Cotter et al., 2013) to draw samples from the posterior distribution of the initial vorticity in Navier-Stokes given sparse, noisy observations at time _T_ = 50. We compare the Fourier neural operator acting as a surrogate model with the traditional solvers used to generate our train-test data (both run on GPU). We generate 25,000 samples from the posterior (with a 5,000 sample burn-in period), requiring 30,000 evaluations of the forward operator.

As shown in Figure 6 (Appendix A.5), FNO and the traditional solver recover almost the same posterior mean which, when pushed forward, recovers well the late-time dynamic of Navier Stokes. In sharp contrast, FNO takes 0 _._ 005 _s_ to evaluate a single instance while the traditional solver, after being optimized to use the largest possible internal time-step which does not lead to blow-up, takes 2 _._ 2 _s_ . This amounts to 2 _._ 5 minutes for the MCMC using FNO and over 18 hours for the traditional solver. Even if we account for data generation and training time (offline steps) which take 12 hours, using FNO is still faster! Once trained, FNO can be used to quickly perform multiple MCMC runs for different initial conditions and observations, while the traditional solver will take 18 hours for every instance. Furthermore, since FNO is differentiable, it can easily be applied to PDE-constrained optimization problems without the need for the adjoint method.

**Spectral analysis.** Due to the way we parameterize _Rφ_ , the function output by (4) has at most _k_ max _,j_ Fourier modes per channel. This, however, does not mean that the Fourier neural operator can only approximate functions up to _k_ max _,j_ modes. Indeed, the activation functions which occur between integral operators and the final decoder network _Q_ recover the high frequency modes. As an example, consider a solution to the Navier-Stokes equation with viscosity _ν_ = 1e _−_ 3. Truncating this function at 20 Fourier modes yields an error around 2% while our Fourier neural operator learns the parametric dependence and produces approximations to an error of _≤_ 1% with only _k_ max _,j_ = 12 parameterized modes.

**Non-periodic boundary condition.** Traditional Fourier methods work only with periodic boundary conditions. However, the Fourier neural operator does not have this limitation. This is due to the linear transform _W_ (the bias term) which keeps the track of non-periodic boundary. As an example, the Darcy Flow and the time domain of Navier-Stokes have non-periodic boundary conditions, and the Fourier neural operator still learns the solution operator with excellent accuracy.

## 6 DISCUSSION AND CONCLUSION

**Requirements on Data.** Data-driven methods rely on the quality and quantity of data. To learn Navier-Stokes equation with Reynolds number _Re_ = 1e+4, we need to generate _N_ = 10000 training pairs _{aj, uj}_ with the numerical solver. However, for more challenging PDEs, generating a few training samples can be already very expensive. A future direction is to combine neural operators with numerical solvers to levitate the requirements on data. **Recurrent structure.** The neural operator has an iterative structure that can naturally be formulated as a recurrent network where all layers share the same parameters without sacrificing performance. (We did not impose this restriction in the experiments.) **Computer vision.** Operator learning is not restricted to PDEs. Images can naturally be viewed as real-valued functions on 2-d domains and videos simply add a temporal structure. Our approach is therefore a natural choice for problems in computer vision where invariance to discretization crucial is important (Chi et al., 2020).

progressive__gemini-3-flash-preview.label: Progressive (gemini-3-flash-preview)
progressive__gemini-3-flash-preview.overall_feedback: This paper presents a significant advancement in scientific machine learning by bridging the gap between operator learning and efficient spectral methods. The Fourier Neural Operator (FNO) addresses a critical bottleneck in existing neural operators—the high computational cost of evaluating integral kernels—by leveraging the Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) to perform operations in the frequency domain. This approach is conceptually elegant and practically impactful, as it inherently allows for mesh-invariance and "zero-shot super-resolution," solving a major limitation of standard CNN-based PDE solvers. The clarity of the introduction is excellent; the authors provide a rigorous taxonomy of existing methods (Finite-dimensional vs. Neural-FEM vs. Neural Operators), which effectively situates their contribution within the literature. The most significant potential issue lies in the reliance on periodic boundary conditions and rectangular domains inherent to the FFT, which may limit the method’s direct applicability to complex, non-periodic geometries common in engineering. However, as a foundational framework for parametric PDEs, the work demonstrates high quality through its combination of theoretical motivation and impressive empirical speedups over traditional numerical solvers.

comments.title: Possible OCR Error or Inconsistency in Observation Dimensionality
comments.quote: assume we have observations $a_j|D_j \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_a}$, $u_j|D_j \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_v}$
comments.explanation: In the section on Discretization, the text defines the observation of the output function $u_j$ at $n$ points as taking values in $\mathbb{R}^{d_v}$. However, in the 'Learning Operators' section and the 'Neural Operator' architectural description, $u$ is consistently defined as taking values in $\mathbb{R}^{d_u}$ (the target dimension), while $d_v$ is reserved for the internal 'higher dimensional representation' or latent channel space after the lifting map $P$. Specifically, Definition 1 states $Q: \mathbb{R}^{d_v} \to \mathbb{R}^{d_u}$. It is highly probable that the OCR misread $d_u$ as $d_v$ here, or the authors used the latent dimension symbol for the output space. While a reader can infer the intent from the context of the architecture, it creates a formal contradiction regarding the space $U$.

comments.title: Ambiguous Iterative Update Equation Mapping
comments.quote: Define the update to the representation $v_t \mapsto v_{t+1}$ by $$v_{t+1}(x) = \sigma\left( W v_t(x) + \int_D \kappa_\phi(x, y, a(x), a(y)) v_t(y) dy \right)$$ (implied from Definitions 1 and 2)
comments.explanation: Definition 1 introduces the update as $v_{t+1} = \sigma(W v_t + K(a; \phi)v_t)$. Definition 2 then defines the kernel integral operator $K$. However, the prose in Section 3 states that $K$ maps to 'bounded linear operators on $U(D; \mathbb{R}^{d_v})$'. In standard operator theory, if $K$ is the integral operator, the variable $a$ usually parameterizes the kernel $\kappa$. However, the placement of $a$ in the signature $K: A \times \Theta_K$ suggests the operator itself depends on the input function $a$. While this is a powerful generalization, the transition to the Fourier Neural Operator (Section 4) simplifies this by 'removing the dependence on the function $a$'. There is a slight logical gap for a reader trying to reconcile how the general non-linear operator $G$ is built: the paper implies $K$ can depend on $a$ (Definition 2), but the actual implementation (Section 4) uses a fixed kernel $\kappa(x-y)$ that does not depend on the specific input $a$ currently being processed (except for the 'Parameterizations of R' note which says they didn't use the $a$-dependent version because it performed worse). This makes the claim 'neural operator requires no knowledge of the underlying PDE' true, but the formal connection between the $a$-dependent integral and the $a$-independent Fourier implementation is slightly underspecified.

comments.title: Complexity Analysis Discrepancy
comments.quote: General Fourier transforms have complexity $O(n^2)$, however, since we truncate the series the complexity is in fact $O(nk_{max})$, while the FFT has complexity $O(n \log n)$.
comments.explanation: The authors claim that because they truncate the Fourier series to $k_{max}$ modes, the complexity of a 'General Fourier Transform' (presumably the Discrete Fourier Transform or a direct summation) is $O(nk_{max})$. While technically true that evaluating $k$ modes at $n$ points takes $O(nk)$ time, the FNO's primary efficiency claim relies on the FFT. However, the FFT calculates all $n$ modes in $O(n \log n)$, and one cannot typically calculate just the low-frequency subset of modes in $O(nk_{max})$ unless using specific algorithms like the Sparse FFT, which are not mentioned. If the authors are using a standard FFT library (as implied by the mention of 'Fast Fourier Transform'), the complexity remains $O(n \log n)$ regardless of $k_{max}$. If they are using a direct summation for the $k_{max}$ modes to maintain 'discretization invariance' on non-uniform grids, the $O(nk_{max})$ applies, but then the comparison to the 'speed advantage' of the FFT is confusing. The text oscillates between justifying the complexity via $k_{max}$ truncation and via the FFT algorithm.

comments.title: Possible Dimensional Inconsistency in Kernel Integral Definition
comments.quote: where $\kappa_\phi : \mathbb{R}^{2(d + d_a)} \to \mathbb{R}^{d_v \times d_v}$ is a neural network parameterized by $\phi \in \Theta_K$.
comments.explanation: In Definition 2, the kernel $\kappa$ is defined as taking inputs from $\mathbb{R}^{2(d + d_a)}$. According to the context of Equation (3), the inputs to $\kappa_\phi$ are $(x, y, a(x), a(y))$. Since $x, y \in D \subset \mathbb{R}^d$ and $a(x), a(y) \in \mathbb{R}^{d_a}$, the total input dimension is $d + d + d_a + d_a = 2d + 2d_a$, which matches the formula. However, the subsequent section in 'Fourier Neural Operator' defines the convolution as $\kappa_\phi(x-y)$. In a standard convolution $\kappa(x-y)$, the kernel depends only on the relative displacement in $\mathbb{R}^d$. If the authors intended to maintain the dependence on the function value $a$, the simplification to a pure convolution $\kappa(x-y)$ in Equation (4) represents a significant reduction in the expressive power originally claimed in Definition 2. While common in CNNs, this shift from a general kernel $G(x,y,a(x),a(y))$ to a displacement-only kernel $G(x-y)$ is a strong assumption (stationarity/homogeneity) that isn't explicitly reconciled with the 'highly non-linear' claims until much later when mentioning $R$ could depend on $Fa$. It may confuse a reader expecting the full non-local dependence described in the general theory.

comments.title: Inconsistent Complexity Analysis for Truncated Fourier Transform
comments.quote: General Fourier transforms have complexity $O(n^2)$, however, since we truncate the series the complexity is in fact $O(nk_{max})$, while the FFT has complexity $O(n \log n)$.
comments.explanation: The authors state that because they truncate the series to $k_{max}$ modes, the complexity of a 'general' Fourier transform is $O(nk_{max})$. While it is true that computing $k_{max}$ coefficients via a Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT) sum takes $O(nk_{max})$, the paper primarily advocates for the use of the FFT to achieve 'quasi-linear' time. Standard FFT algorithms (like FFTW used in most deep learning frameworks) compute all $n$ modes in $O(n \log n)$ time. You cannot typically compute a subset of $k_{max}$ modes using an FFT algorithm to get a complexity of $O(nk_{max})$ unless $k_{max} < \log n$, which is rarely the case for 2D/3D PDE problems (where $n$ is large but $k_{max}$ is a significant 'corner' of the frequency box). In practice, if one uses the FFT, the complexity is $O(n \log n)$, and the truncation happens *after* the transform. The $O(nk_{max})$ figure only applies if one avoids the FFT and uses a direct sum for the restricted modes. This creates an ambiguity about the actual performance bottleneck in the provided implementation.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'Zero-Shot' Super-Resolution Definition
comments.quote: The Fourier neural operator is the first ML-based method to successfully model turbulent flows with zero-shot super-resolution.
comments.explanation: The term 'zero-shot super-resolution' is used to claim that the model can be evaluated on a higher resolution than used in training. However, the paper acknowledges that the Fourier method requires a 'uniform discretization' for the FFT to be used efficiently. In the 'Invariance to discretization' section, the authors claim the basis $e^{2\pi i \langle x, k \rangle}$ is well-defined everywhere. This implies that if a model is trained on a $64 \times 64$ grid, evaluating it on $256 \times 256$ is 'zero-shot'. However, since the model only learns up to $k_{max}$ modes (e.g., 12 or 16), evaluating on a higher resolution does not actually recover higher-frequency physics (super-resolution in the classical sense); it simply interpolates the lower frequencies using the trigonometric basis. In the context of turbulent flows (Navier-Stokes), where energy exists in high-frequency modes, a model truncated at $k_{max}=12$ will inherently act as a low-pass filter. The claim that it 'models turbulent flows' at higher resolutions might be misinterpreted as the model 'discovering' or 'predicting' high-frequency spectral content that was absent in the training data, when it is actually performing a high-quality trigonometric interpolation of the filtered solution.

comments.title: Index Confusion in Fourier Integral Operator Multiplication
comments.quote: (R \cdot \hat{v}_t)_{k,l} = \sum_{j=1}^{d_v} R_{k,l,j} \hat{v}_t(k,j)
comments.explanation: In Definition 3 and the subsequent 'Discrete Multiplication' definition in the summary, the multiplication of the weight tensor $R$ with the Fourier coefficients $\hat{v}_t$ is defined as $(R \cdot \hat{v}_t)_{k,l} = \sum_{j=1}^{d_v} R_{k,l,j} \hat{v}_t(k,j)$. However, based on the dimensions provided ($R \in \mathbb{C}^{k_{max} \times d_v \times d_v}$ and $\hat{v}_t \in \mathbb{C}^{k_{max} \times d_v}$), the index $l$ on the left-hand side suggests the output channel, but the tensor $R$ is indexed as $R_{k,l,j}$. Usually, for a matrix-vector product at each frequency $k$, we expect the summation to occur over the input channel index. If $j$ is the input channel and $l$ is the output channel, $R$ should technically be indexed as $R_{k,l,j}$ (mapping channel $j$ to $l$ at mode $k$). While the formula is internally consistent as a contraction, the notation $\hat{v}_t(k,j)$ uses $k$ as a multi-index for frequency and $j$ for channel, which is standard, but the tensor $R$ dimensions are later described as $(k_{max} \times d_v \times d_v)$. A careful reader might be confused as to whether $R$ is shared across modes or if each mode has a unique $d_v \times d_v$ matrix. The text clarifies $R$ is a 'complex-valued weight tensor', implying the latter. However, the order of indices in $R_{k,l,j}$ versus the dimension description could be clearer to ensure it corresponds to standard batch matrix multiplication.

comments.title: Truncated Mode Set Definition Inconsistency
comments.quote: Z_{k_{max}} = \{ (k_1, \dots, k_d) \in \mathbb{Z}_{s_1} \times \dots \times \mathbb{Z}_{s_d} | k_j \leq k_{max,j} \text{ or } s_j - k_j \leq k_{max,j}, \text{ for } j = 1, \dots, d \}.
comments.explanation: The authors define the set of truncated modes for the FFT implementation to include both low frequencies and high frequencies (the 'corners' of the DFT output). In the standard DFT layout (like `fftn` in NumPy/PyTorch), the low frequencies are at the start and end of the array due to periodicity (0 to $k_{max}$ and $s-k_{max}$ to $s-1$). However, in Section 4, they state: 'a linear transform $R$ on the lower Fourier modes and filters out the higher modes'. By including the modes where $s_j - k_j \leq k_{max,j}$, they are correctly capturing the negative frequency components of the low-frequency spectrum. However, there is a small logical overlap: if $k_{max}$ is large relative to $s$, these sets could overlap. More importantly, the notation $k_j \leq k_{max,j}$ usually implies $0 \leq k_j$, but the indices for $k$ in the FFT sum are defined as $k_j=0, \dots, s_j-1$. While a practitioner would understand this refers to the DC and low-frequency components, the formal set definition is slightly loose regarding the 'middle' frequencies which are being discarded. It's an accurate description of the 'corners', but calling them just 'lower' modes in the text is a simplification that ignores how the DFT stores negative frequencies.

comments.title: Unclear Kernel Dependence on the Parameter 'a'
comments.quote: (K(a; \phi)v_t)(x) = \int_D \kappa_\phi (x, y, a(x), a(y)) v_t(y) dy
comments.explanation: Definition 2 (Kernel integral operator) explicitly includes the dependence of the kernel $\kappa$ on the input function $a$ at points $x$ and $y$. However, when moving to the Fourier Neural Operator (Section 4), the authors state: 'If we remove the dependence on the function $a$ and impose $\kappa_\phi(x,y) = \kappa_\phi(x-y)$, we obtain ... a convolution operator.' They then proceed to parameterize $R$ directly as a weight tensor independent of $a$. While Section 4 mentions they 'experimented with linear as well as neural network parameterizations of $R_\phi$' that depend on $(Fa)$, the primary FNO architecture described and benchmarked assumes a kernel $\kappa$ that does not depend on $a$ (i.e., it is the same convolution kernel for every input $a$). This is a significant simplification of the 'Neural Operator' framework. The transition from the general $K(a)$ to the $a$-independent $K$ in FNO is logically sound but undercuts the claim in the introduction that FNO 'learns the mapping from any functional parametric dependence'. The kernel itself becomes independent of the parameter $a$, and the non-linearity enters only through the lifting $P$, the bias $W$, and the activations $\sigma$. This should be more explicitly flagged as a restricted case of the general operator theory presented in Section 2.

comments.title: Discrepancy in Navier-Stokes Performance Interpretation
comments.quote: For the configurations where the amount of data is insufficient (\nu = 1e-4, N = 1000 and \nu = 1e-5, N = 1000), all methods have > 15% error with FNO-2D achieving the lowest.
comments.explanation:
In Table 1, for $
u = 1e-4, N = 1000$, the FNO-2D error is listed as 0.1918 (19.18%), while U-Net is 0.2051, and TF-Net is 0.2253. While FNO-2D is indeed the lowest, the '30% lower' claim in the 'Key Claims' (and abstract/intro) seems to rely specifically on the high-data or lower-viscosity regimes ($1e-3$). In the more challenging turbulent regimes ($1e-4$, $1e-5$) with limited data, the margins between FNO-2D and standard CNN architectures like U-Net shrink significantly. Furthermore, the claim that 'FNO-3D has the best performance when there is sufficient data' is supported by the 0.0086 error for $
u=1e-3, N=1000$, but FNO-3D's performance on $
u=1e-4, N=1000$ (0.1918) is actually identical to or slightly worse than FNO-2D in some contexts (though FNO-2D is not shown for the $N=10000$ case where 3D shines). The text claims FNO-2D achieves the lowest error in low-data regimes, but the table shows FNO-3D is actually not listed for all combinations, making the 'best' claim slightly ambiguous across the different FNO variants.

However, the subsequent section in 'Fourier Neural Operator' defines the convolution as $\kappa_\phi(x-y)$. In a standard convolution $\kappa(x-y)$, the kernel depends only on the relative displacement in $\mathbb{R}^d$. If the authors intended to maintain the dependence on the function value $a$, the simplification to a pure convolution $\kappa(x-y)$ in Equation (4) represents a significant reduction in the expressive power originally claimed in Definition 2. While common in CNNs, this shift from a general kernel $G(x,y,a(x),a(y))$ to a displacement-only kernel $G(x-y)$ is a strong assumption (stationarity/homogeneity) that isn't explicitly reconciled with the 'highly non-linear' claims until much later when mentioning $R$ could depend on $Fa$. It may confuse a reader expecting the full non-local dependence described in the general theory.

comments.title: Inconsistent Complexity Analysis for Truncated Fourier Transform
comments.quote: General Fourier transforms have complexity $O(n^2)$, however, since we truncate the series the complexity is in fact $O(nk_{max})$, while the FFT has complexity $O(n \log n)$.
comments.explanation: The authors state that because they truncate the series to $k_{max}$ modes, the complexity of a 'general' Fourier transform is $O(nk_{max})$. While it is true that computing $k_{max}$ coefficients via a Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT) sum takes $O(nk_{max})$, the paper primarily advocates for the use of the FFT to achieve 'quasi-linear' time. Standard FFT algorithms (like FFTW used in most deep learning frameworks) compute all $n$ modes in $O(n \log n)$ time. You cannot typically compute a subset of $k_{max}$ modes using an FFT algorithm to get a complexity of $O(nk_{max})$ unless $k_{max} < \log n$, which is rarely the case for 2D/3D PDE problems (where $n$ is large but $k_{max}$ is a significant 'corner' of the frequency box). In practice, if one uses the FFT, the complexity is $O(n \log n)$, and the truncation happens *after* the transform. The $O(nk_{max})$ figure only applies if one avoids the FFT and uses a direct sum for the restricted modes. This creates an ambiguity about the actual performance bottleneck in the provided implementation.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'Zero-Shot' Super-Resolution Definition
comments.quote: The Fourier neural operator is the first ML-based method to successfully model turbulent flows with zero-shot super-resolution.
comments.explanation: The term 'zero-shot super-resolution' is used to claim that the model can be evaluated on a higher resolution than used in training. However, the paper acknowledges that the Fourier method requires a 'uniform discretization' for the FFT to be used efficiently. In the 'Invariance to discretization' section, the authors claim the basis $e^{2\pi i \langle x, k \rangle}$ is well-defined everywhere. This implies that if a model is trained on a $64 \times 64$ grid, evaluating it on $256 \times 256$ is 'zero-shot'. However, since the model only learns up to $k_{max}$ modes (e.g., 12 or 16), evaluating on a higher resolution does not actually recover higher-frequency physics (super-resolution in the classical sense); it simply interpolates the lower frequencies using the trigonometric basis. In the context of turbulent flows (Navier-Stokes), where energy exists in high-frequency modes, a model truncated at $k_{max}=12$ will inherently act as a low-pass filter. The claim that it 'models turbulent flows' at higher resolutions might be misinterpreted as the model 'discovering' or 'predicting' high-frequency spectral content that was absent in the training data, when it is actually performing a high-quality trigonometric interpolation of the filtered solution.

comments.title: Index Confusion in Fourier Integral Operator Multiplication
comments.quote: (R \cdot \hat{v}_t)_{k,l} = \sum_{j=1}^{d_v} R_{k,l,j} \hat{v}_t(k,j)
comments.explanation: In Definition 3 and the subsequent 'Discrete Multiplication' definition in the summary, the multiplication of the weight tensor $R$ with the Fourier coefficients $\hat{v}_t$ is defined as $(R \cdot \hat{v}_t)_{k,l} = \sum_{j=1}^{d_v} R_{k,l,j} \hat{v}_t(k,j)$. However, based on the dimensions provided ($R \in \mathbb{C}^{k_{max} \times d_v \times d_v}$ and $\hat{v}_t \in \mathbb{C}^{k_{max} \times d_v}$), the index $l$ on the left-hand side suggests the output channel, but the tensor $R$ is indexed as $R_{k,l,j}$. Usually, for a matrix-vector product at each frequency $k$, we expect the summation to occur over the input channel index. If $j$ is the input channel and $l$ is the output channel, $R$ should technically be indexed as $R_{k,l,j}$ (mapping channel $j$ to $l$ at mode $k$). While the formula is internally consistent as a contraction, the notation $\hat{v}_t(k,j)$ uses $k$ as a multi-index for frequency and $j$ for channel, which is standard, but the tensor $R$ dimensions are later described as $(k_{max} \times d_v \times d_v)$. A careful reader might be confused as to whether $R$ is shared across modes or if each mode has a unique $d_v \times d_v$ matrix. The text clarifies $R$ is a 'complex-valued weight tensor', implying the latter. However, the order of indices in $R_{k,l,j}$ versus the dimension description could be clearer to ensure it corresponds to standard batch matrix multiplication.

comments.title: Truncated Mode Set Definition Inconsistency
comments.quote: Z_{k_{max}} = \{ (k_1, \dots, k_d) \in \mathbb{Z}_{s_1} \times \dots \times \mathbb{Z}_{s_d} | k_j \leq k_{max,j} \text{ or } s_j - k_j \leq k_{max,j}, \text{ for } j = 1, \dots, d \}.
comments.explanation: The authors define the set of truncated modes for the FFT implementation to include both low frequencies and high frequencies (the 'corners' of the DFT output). In the standard DFT layout (like `fftn` in NumPy/PyTorch), the low frequencies are at the start and end of the array due to periodicity (0 to $k_{max}$ and $s-k_{max}$ to $s-1$). However, in Section 4, they state: 'a linear transform $R$ on the lower Fourier modes and filters out the higher modes'. By including the modes where $s_j - k_j \leq k_{max,j}$, they are correctly capturing the negative frequency components of the low-frequency spectrum. However, there is a small logical overlap: if $k_{max}$ is large relative to $s$, these sets could overlap. More importantly, the notation $k_j \leq k_{max,j}$ usually implies $0 \leq k_j$, but the indices for $k$ in the FFT sum are defined as $k_j=0, \dots, s_j-1$. While a practitioner would understand this refers to the DC and low-frequency components, the formal set definition is slightly loose regarding the 'middle' frequencies which are being discarded. It's an accurate description of the 'corners', but calling them just 'lower' modes in the text is a simplification that ignores how the DFT stores negative frequencies.

comments.title: Unclear Kernel Dependence on the Parameter 'a'
comments.quote: (K(a; \phi)v_t)(x) = \int_D \kappa_\phi (x, y, a(x), a(y)) v_t(y) dy
comments.explanation: Definition 2 (Kernel integral operator) explicitly includes the dependence of the kernel $\kappa$ on the input function $a$ at points $x$ and $y$. However, when moving to the Fourier Neural Operator (Section 4), the authors state: 'If we remove the dependence on the function $a$ and impose $\kappa_\phi(x,y) = \kappa_\phi(x-y)$, we obtain ... a convolution operator.' They then proceed to parameterize $R$ directly as a weight tensor independent of $a$. While Section 4 mentions they 'experimented with linear as well as neural network parameterizations of $R_\phi$' that depend on $(Fa)$, the primary FNO architecture described and benchmarked assumes a kernel $\kappa$ that does not depend on $a$ (i.e., it is the same convolution kernel for every input $a$). This is a significant simplification of the 'Neural Operator' framework. The transition from the general $K(a)$ to the $a$-independent $K$ in FNO is logically sound but undercuts the claim in the introduction that FNO 'learns the mapping from any functional parametric dependence'. The kernel itself becomes independent of the parameter $a$, and the non-linearity enters only through the lifting $P$, the bias $W$, and the activations $\sigma$. This should be more explicitly flagged as a restricted case of the general operator theory presented in Section 2.

comments.title: Discrepancy in Navier-Stokes Performance Interpretation
comments.quote: For the configurations where the amount of data is insufficient (\nu = 1e-4, N = 1000 and \nu = 1e-5, N = 1000), all methods have > 15% error with FNO-2D achieving the lowest.
comments.explanation:
In Table 1, for $
u = 1e-4, N = 1000$, the FNO-2D error is listed as 0.1918 (19.18%), while U-Net is 0.2051, and TF-Net is 0.2253. While FNO-2D is indeed the lowest, the '30% lower' claim in the 'Key Claims' (and abstract/intro) seems to rely specifically on the high-data or lower-viscosity regimes ($1e-3$). In the more challenging turbulent regimes ($1e-4$, $1e-5$) with limited data, the margins between FNO-2D and standard CNN architectures like U-Net shrink significantly. Furthermore, the claim that 'FNO-3D has the best performance when there is sufficient data' is supported by the 0.0086 error for $
u=1e-3, N=1000$, but FNO-3D's performance on $
u=1e-4, N=1000$ (0.1918) is actually identical to or slightly worse than FNO-2D in some contexts (though FNO-2D is not shown for the $N=10000$ case where 3D shines). The text claims FNO-2D achieves the lowest error in low-data regimes, but the table shows FNO-3D is actually not listed for all combinations, making the 'best' claim slightly ambiguous across the different FNO variants.

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: This introduction demonstrates high academic rigor and structural clarity, effectively positioning the Fourier Neural Operator (FNO) within the broader landscape of computational physics and machine learning. The authors do an excellent job of distilling the limitations of existing paradigms—specifically contrasting the resolution-dependency of finite-dimensional operators with the computational overhead of Neural-FEM—to articulate a clear, compelling motivation for their proposed method. By identifying the specific "bottleneck" of integral operator evaluation and proposing the Fourier transform as an elegant computational solution, the paper establishes a strong sense of technical necessity. The writing is highly professional, logically sequenced, and sets a high bar for experimental evaluation by promising (and contextualizing) significant gains in both speed and resolution-invariance. Overall, the text is exceptionally well-oriented, though the depth of the subsequent technical sections will be critical to ensuring the transition from intuition to implementation maintains this high standard of accessibility.

comments.title: Inconsistent and ambiguous notation for Fourier mode truncation
comments.quote: k_max = |Zk_max | = |{k ∈ Z^d : |kj| ≤ k_max ,j}|
comments.explanation: The text conflates the total count of modes (cardinality) with the per-dimension truncation threshold, using 'k_max' for both. Additionally, the definition of the index set Z_kmax in paragraph 42 is mathematically imprecise regarding whether it refers to standard frequency indices, absolute values, or FFT-shifted indices. This creates confusion regarding both the dimensionality of the weight tensor R_φ and the actual number of modes being parameterized, making implementation ambiguous.

comments.title: Unclear dimensionality and input specification for the kernel κφ
comments.quote: where κφ: R[2(] d[+] da[)] → R dv[×]dv
comments.explanation: The notation for the kernel domain is OCR-degraded and mathematically underspecified. Furthermore, it is unclear if the operator K(a; φ) is intended to be conditioned on the input 'a' at the Fourier layer level, as the notation suggests dependence, but the implementation appears to be a parameter-independent convolution. The lack of clarity on whether the operator is a general integral kernel or a fixed convolution complicates interpretation.

comments.title: Misleading terminology regarding temporal 'zero-shot' super-resolution
comments.quote: And surprisingly, it can do super-resolution not only in the spatial domain but also in the temporal domain.
comments.explanation: The claim of 'zero-shot' super-resolution in the temporal domain is potentially inaccurate. Since the model relies on FFT integration over time, changing the temporal resolution/sequence length is an extrapolation task. Without theoretical support for discretization-invariance in the temporal Fourier layers, equating this to spatial zero-shot super-resolution is misleading.

comments.title: Misaligned and redundant table header
comments.explanation: Table 1 column headers contain redundant values and appear to merge the N=1000 and N=10000 scenarios. While the numerical data may be consistent, the labeling prevents a clear reader mapping between simulation parameters and results.

progressive_original__gemini-3.1-flash-l.label: Progressive Original (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive_original__gemini-3.1-flash-l.overall_feedback: This introduction demonstrates high academic rigor and structural clarity, effectively positioning the Fourier Neural Operator (FNO) within the broader landscape of computational physics and machine learning. The authors do an excellent job of distilling the limitations of existing paradigms—specifically contrasting the resolution-dependency of finite-dimensional operators with the computational overhead of Neural-FEM—to articulate a clear, compelling motivation for their proposed method. By identifying the specific "bottleneck" of integral operator evaluation and proposing the Fourier transform as an elegant computational solution, the paper establishes a strong sense of technical necessity. The writing is highly professional, logically sequenced, and sets a high bar for experimental evaluation by promising (and contextualizing) significant gains in both speed and resolution-invariance. Overall, the text is exceptionally well-oriented, though the depth of the subsequent technical sections will be critical to ensuring the transition from intuition to implementation maintains this high standard of accessibility.

comments.title: Ambiguous index in Definition 1
comments.explanation: In Definition 1 and the surrounding text, the authors introduce an iterative update vt -> vt+1 where t moves from 0 to T-1. However, the text immediately preceding the definition introduces the sequence notation 'vj for j = 0, 1, ..., T-1'. This notation is potentially confusing as 'j' is used both as the index for the sequence step in the informal intro and then replaced by 't' in the specific update formula. While a reader can infer that 'vj' and 'vt' refer to the same sequence of hidden representations, the inconsistency between the narrative introduction ('vj') and the formal definition ('vt') is suboptimal.

comments.title: Unclear dimensionality of the kernel κφ
comments.quote: where κφ: R[2(] d[+] da[)] → R dv[×]dv
comments.explanation: The OCR correctly captured the messiness of the text here: 'R[2(] d[+] da[)]'. Given the context that the kernel function κφ(x, y, a(x), a(y)) takes inputs from the spatial domain D and the function space A, the domain should be 2d + 2da (spatial coordinates x and y, and values a(x) and a(y)). The current notation 'd + da' inside the parentheses seems to imply a single x and a(x), but the actual integral in Definition 2 depends on both x and y. While I understand the author's intent (mapping kernel input space to the weights), the expression is mathematically underspecified due to the likely OCR-degraded parentheses and exponent.

comments.title: Inconsistent definition of k_max
comments.quote: We pick a finite-dimensional parameterization by truncating the Fourier series at a maximal number of modes k_max = |Zk_max | = |{k ∈ Z^d : |kj| ≤ k_max ,j, for j = 1, . . . , d}|.
comments.explanation: The notation suggests k_max is a scalar (a count of modes), but the set definition {k ∈ Z^d : |kj| ≤ k_max} defines a hypercube of side-length 2k_max + 1. The text calculates this as |Zk_max| = k_max^d (or implies it scale as such), but the cardinality of that set is actually (2k_max + 1)^d. While the authors later define k_max,j to allow for directional truncation, the initial definition is mathematically ambiguous and inconsistent with the cardinality expression provided.

comments.title: Ambiguity in weight tensor dimension R
comments.quote: We thus parameterize R_φ directly as complex-valued (k_max × dv × dv)-tensor
comments.explanation: The text states R_φ is a (k_max × dv × dv)-tensor. However, in the discrete case, the Fourier modes are multi-dimensional (indexed by k=(k_1, ..., k_d)). If R_φ acts on the Fourier coefficients, it must match the dimensions of F(v_t). Given the definition of Zk_max as a set in Z^d, R_φ should be a (d_v × d_v × |Zk_max|)-tensor. Referring to it as a (k_max × dv × dv)-tensor is dimensionally inconsistent with the previous definition of k_max as a total count.

comments.title: Potential conflict between definition of k_max and implementation
comments.quote: k_max = 12^d parameters per channel
comments.explanation: In Section 4, the authors define k_max for the 2-d case as k_max=12. If k_max is the truncation mode index (as implied by |k_j| ≤ k_max), the number of modes is (2*12 + 1)^2 = 625. If the total number of modes is 12^d = 144, the truncation index must be significantly smaller. The prose transitions between describing k_max as a 'maximal mode index' and a 'total count' without sufficient clarity, which might confuse a reader trying to implement this.

comments.title: Inconsistency in Fourier integral operator definition
comments.quote: K(a; φ)vt(x) = F−1[Rφ · F(vt)](x)
comments.explanation: The equation defines the operator K as acting on the function vt (the hidden representation), but the notation K(a; φ) implies an explicit dependence on the input/parameter 'a'. According to Definition 2, the integral kernel κ depends on 'a(x), a(y)'. However, in Definition 3 and subsequent sections, the dependence on 'a' within the operator Rφ is dropped, making the integral operator effectively a linear convolution independent of 'a' in the Fourier layer itself. While the authors remark later that they *can* define Rφ to depend on (Fa), it remains unclear if the notation K(a; φ) is a vestige of the general kernel form (Def 2) or if the Fourier layer is intended to be conditioned on 'a' in the specific implementation discussed. The notation K(a; φ) is misleading given that the actual implemented Fourier layers are parameter-independent kernels.

comments.title: Ambiguous truncation set definitions
comments.quote: Zkmax = { (k1, ..., kd) ∈ Zs1 × · · · × Zsd | kj ≤ kmax,j or sj − kj ≤ kmax,j, for j = 1, ..., d}
comments.explanation: The OR condition in the definition of Zkmax suggests a rectangular truncation in the Fourier domain (taking the 'corners' of the frequency spectrum). However, the preceding text says 'the low frequency modes are usually defined by placing an upper-bound on the ℓ1-norm of k'. If the set is intended to capture low frequencies, the definition using the corners (periodic boundary behavior of DFT) is only one way to interpret 'lower modes'. While likely a standard choice to accommodate the FFT's storage of negative frequencies, the logic 'k_j ≤ k_max,j or s_j - k_j ≤ k_max,j' is slightly ambiguous regarding whether this encompasses all indices below a certain threshold or just the frequencies near the origin and Nyquist frequency.

comments.title: Mismatch in Table 1 column headers
comments.explanation: In Table 1, the columns for Navier-Stokes error are labeled with four conditions, but the third column header is 'ν = 1e−4 T = 30 N = 10000' and the second is 'ν = 1e−4 T = 30 N = 1000'. The values in the table for those columns are consistent, but the table header formatting includes a repeating redundant label ('ν = 1e−4 T = 30') and seems to have merged or misaligned the 'N=' counts for the second and third scenario, creating confusion about which column corresponds to N=1000 vs N=10000.

comments.title: Inconsistency in Fourier Mode Indexing Definition
comments.quote: Zk max = { ( k1 , . . . , kd ) ∈ Z s1 × · · · × Z sd | kj ≤ kmax ,j or sj − kj ≤ kmax ,j , for j = 1 , . . . , d }.
comments.explanation: In Section 4, the definition of the truncated Fourier modes is given using a condition involving '|' which reads as absolute values, but the text later defines the index set using inequalities like 'kj ≤ kmax,j'. The notation 'kj ≤ kmax,j or sj − kj ≤ kmax,j' is a standard description of the set of indices corresponding to low-frequency corners in an FFT-shifted array. However, the use of '<=' without mentioning absolute values for the condition on 'kj' or the modular arithmetic implied for the 'sj - kj' term is mathematically ambiguous. While a reader familiar with FFT implementations will likely interpret this as the set of indices {0, ..., kmax} and {sj - kmax, ..., sj - 1}, the provided LaTeX-extracted text is technically underspecified for a formal definition, potentially leading to confusion regarding whether indices can be negative.

comments.title: Claim of 'Zero-shot' Super-Resolution on Temporal Domain
comments.quote: And surprisingly, it can do super-resolution not only in the spatial domain but also in the temporal domain.
comments.explanation: The paper claims the model is 'zero-shot' in the temporal domain. However, FNO-3d (as defined in the context) propagates the whole time series at once, and the architecture often depends on a fixed sequence length or discretization of the time interval in the FFT/integral operator layers. If the network is trained on $T=20$ and evaluated on $T=80$, this is 'extrapolation' in the temporal dimension. The term 'zero-shot super-resolution' is typically reserved for spatial grid refinement where the operator is discretization-invariant. Extending this to time is a strong claim that lacks theoretical backing in the text regarding how the integral/convolution kernel handles temporal frequencies when the input sequence length change is significant.

comments.title: Ambiguity of Input/Output dimensions in Fourier layer definition
comments.quote: We pick a finite-dimensional parameterization by truncating the Fourier series at a maximal number of modes k max = |Zk max | = |{k ∈ Z d : |kj| ≤ k max ,j , for j = 1 , . . . , d}|.
comments.explanation: There is a minor but confusing notation conflict. The text defines $k_{max}$ as a set cardinality (the number of modes) in the first half of the sentence, but then uses $k_{max,j}$ (a vector or set of scalars) as an index limit in the definition of $Z_{k_{max}}$. This conflates the total count of modes with the truncation threshold per dimension. While the intent is clear to a developer implementing the code, the notation $k_{max} = |Z_{k_{max}}|$ is technically a circular definition or a misuse of $k_{max}$ as both a tally and a threshold.

progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Progressive (deepseek-v4-flash)
progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: This is a high-quality paper with clear exposition and a compelling contribution: introducing a mesh-invariant neural operator architecture that leverages the Fast Fourier Transform to achieve significant computational speedups over traditional solvers and prior ML approaches for parametric PDEs. The clarity is strong overall, with a well-structured introduction that situates the work relative to finite-dimensional operators and Neural-FEM methods. However, several issues warrant attention. First, the claim of being "the first ML-based method to successfully model turbulent flows with zero-shot super-resolution" is bold and may overstate novelty; a more precise comparison with related operator learning approaches (e.g., DeepONet) in terms of resolution transfer capabilities would strengthen the paper. Second, the architecture’s reliance on the Fourier transform implicitly assumes periodic boundary conditions and uniform grids, which limits its generality to more complex domains or non-uniform meshes—this limitation is only briefly hinted at and deserves a more thorough discussion. Third, while the paper demonstrates superior accuracy and speed, a rigorous error analysis (e.g., convergence guarantees with respect to discretization parameters) is lacking, which would bolster the theoretical foundation. Additionally, the experimental sections (not fully provided here) should carefully control for confounding factors such as training data distribution and hyperparameter sensitivity. Overall, the work is innovative and well-written, but addressing these issues would improve its depth and generalizability.

comments.title: Incomplete text and potential missing definition at equation numbers
comments.quote: with quasi-linear time complexity and state-of-the-art approximation capabilities.
comments.explanation: The sentence cuts off abruptly at the end of the Introduction section, and the paper text seems to be truncated at the end of the provided passage. This might just be a PDF extraction boundary issue. However, the passage ends mid-sentence after describing the Fourier neural operator, suggesting the full context is not available for a complete review. Since this appears to be an OCR artifact and not an error in the original paper, I flag it only as a note.

comments.title: Potential confusion between 'Neural-FEM' and 'Neural-FEM' terminology
comments.quote: The second approach directly parameterizes the solution function as a neural network (E & Yu, 2018; Raissi et al., 2019; Bar & Sochen, 2019; Smith et al., 2020; Pan & Duraisamy, 2020). This approach is designed to model one specific instance of the PDE, not the solution operator. It is mesh-independent and accurate, but for any given new instance of the functional parameter/coefficient, it requires training a new neural network. The approach closely resembles classical methods such as finite elements, replacing the linear span of a finite set of local basis functions with the space of neural networks.
comments.explanation: The term 'Neural-FEM' is introduced in the overview section. The authors seem to use it as a label for methods that directly parameterize the solution function as a neural network. However, the cited references (E & Yu, 2018; Raissi et al., 2019) are typically associated with Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs), not with finite element concepts per se. The parallel to finite elements (replacing local basis functions with neural networks) is somewhat loose. While the authors correctly note that such methods require retraining for each new parameter instance, calling them 'Neural-FEM' might mislead readers into thinking they specifically build on Galerkin finite element structure. The name is not standard in the literature, and the description might cause confusion. However, since the authors explicitly explain what they mean, this is a minor framing concern rather than a real error.

comments.title: Missing justification for truncating Fourier modes and choice of k_max
comments.quote: We pick a finite-dimensional parameterization by truncating the Fourier series at a maximal number of modes k_max = |Z_{k_max}| = |{k ∈ Z^d : |k_j| ≤ k_{max,j}, for j = 1,...,d}|. We thus parameterize R_φ directly as complex-valued (k_max × d_v × d_v)-tensor... We note that the set Z_{k_max} is not the canonical choice for the low frequency modes of v_t. Indeed, the low frequency modes are usually defined by placing an upper-bound on the ℓ1-norm of k ∈ Z^d. We choose Z_{k_max} as above since it allows for an efficient implementation.
comments.explanation: The authors acknowledge that the truncation using a component-wise bound (|k_j| ≤ k_{max,j}) is non-canonical compared to an ℓ1-ball truncation, but claim it allows efficient implementation. They do not justify why this specific truncation (which retains 'corner' high-frequency modes in certain directions while discarding low-frequency modes along diagonals) does not compromise accuracy or lead to aliasing issues. Given that the Fourier layer performs a truncated convolution, the choice of truncation pattern directly affects which frequency components are preserved. The paper provides no analysis or experimental comparison to justify this choice. While the results are state-of-the-art, the lack of justification for an important design decision is a methodological gap.

comments.title: Questionable claim about 'first ML-based method to successfully model turbulent flows with zero-shot super-resolution'
comments.quote: The Fourier neural operator is the first ML-based method to successfully model turbulent flows with zero-shot super-resolution.
comments.explanation: This claim appears in the abstract and is also alluded to in the contributions list. The paper provides experimental evidence for zero-shot super-resolution on Navier-Stokes with Reynolds number 10000. However, calling this 'the first ML-based method' to achieve this is a strong historical claim that the paper does not substantiate with a thorough literature review. Prior work (e.g., super-resolution techniques for turbulent flows using GANs or physics-informed upscaling, or other neural operator architectures) might have achieved similar capabilities, even if not framed exactly the same way. Without a comprehensive comparison, this claim may overstate the novelty. Additionally, the Reynolds number 10000 turbulence regime is challenging, but the paper does not quantify what 'successfully model' means in terms of statistical metrics (e.g., energy spectra, correlation functions) versus visual similarity. The claim should be softened or accompanied by explicit comparisons to relevant prior work.

comments.title: Potential inconsistency in the description of the kernel integral operator's input dependence
comments.quote: If we remove the dependence on the function a and impose κ_φ(x,y) = κ_φ(x-y), we obtain that (3) is a convolution operator...
comments.explanation: In Definition 2, the kernel κ_φ is defined to have inputs (x, y, a(x), a(y)), i.e., it depends on the input function a at the evaluation points. The authors then say 'if we remove the dependence on the function a and impose κ_φ(x,y) = κ_φ(x-y)...' This correctly describes the specialization to a translation-invariant kernel. However, in the Fourier neural operator definition (Definition 3), they set R_φ to be the Fourier transform of a periodic function κ: D̅ → R^{d_v×d_v}, which is not explicitly stated to depend on a. In the 'Parameterizations of R' subsection, they mention an option to have R depend on (Fa), but the default (and used in experiments) is independent of a. This leap from the general form (dependent on a) to the Fourier-specific form (independent of a) is glossed over. The reader might wonder whether the Fourier neural operator loses the ability to handle non-translation-invariant kernels or input-dependent kernels that the original neural operator framework allowed. While the authors note this limitation and experiment with alternatives, the justification for why the translation-invariant (a-independent) version works well for PDE problems is missing. This is not a critical error but a missing discussion point.

comments.title: Questionable claim about prior graph neural operators not converging
comments.quote: The Fourier neural operator is the first work that learns the resolution-invariant solution operator for the family of Navier-Stokes equation in the turbulent regime, where previous graph-based neural operators do not converge.
comments.explanation: The assertion that previous graph-based neural operators 'do not converge' is strong and potentially imprecise. Without specifying whether this refers to training convergence, operator convergence, or accuracy on turbulent flows, the claim could mislead readers about the capabilities of prior methods. The paper may provide evidence later (e.g., in experiments), but as stated in the contributions section, it appears as an unqualified dismissal of an entire class of approaches. Given that the text is from an introductory summary, I lean toward leniency, but the ambiguity remains a notable framing concern.

comments.title: Unqualified outperformance claim missing baseline specification
comments.quote: The proposed method consistently outperforms all existing deep learning methods even when fixing the resolution to be 64 _×_ 64. It achieves error rates that are 30% lower on Burgers’ Equation, 60% lower on Darcy Flow, and 30% lower on Navier Stokes (turbulent regime with Reynolds number 10000).
comments.explanation: Stating that the method outperforms 'all existing deep learning methods' is an absolute claim that goes beyond the finite set of baselines actually compared (e.g., NN, RBM, FC, GNO). Additionally, the percentage reductions are given without specifying the reference baseline (best competitor) or the error metric (e.g., relative L2 error). While the contributions section is expected to be promotional, such phrasing could mislead readers into overestimating the breadth of the comparison. The omission of the baseline and metric makes the quantitative claims difficult to interpret without later context.

comments.title: Inconsistent number of Fourier modes in truncation claim
comments.quote: In practice, we have found that choosing k_max,j = 12 which yields k_max = 12^d parameters per channel to be sufficient for all the tasks that we consider.
comments.explanation: The text states that setting k_max,j = 12 yields k_max = 12^d parameters per channel. This directly contradicts the earlier definitions in the same section. In the continuous Fourier series truncation, Z_{k_max} is defined as {k ∈ Z^d : |k_j| ≤ k_max,j}, which has cardinality (2·k_max,j+1)^d. For k_max,j = 12, that is 25^d, not 12^d. In the discrete FFT case, the set of truncated modes is defined differently (the 'corners'), and its size is roughly (2·k_max,j)^d = 24^d for large s_j. Even if conjugate symmetry is exploited to store only half the modes, the number of independent complex parameters would be approximately (k_max,j+1)^d = 13^d. None of these equal 12^d. The claim appears to be an author error rather than an OCR misread, and it could mislead readers about the actual number of parameters in the Fourier layer, affecting reproducibility and understanding of the model's complexity.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of linear transform W as a bias term
comments.quote: This is due to the linear transform W (the bias term) which keeps the track of non-periodic boundary.
comments.explanation: In Definition 1 of the paper, W is a linear transformation mapping ℝ^{d_v} → ℝ^{d_v} (a matrix multiplication), not a bias term. A bias in neural networks is an additive vector, not a matrix. Calling W 'the bias term' is technically incorrect and could mislead a reader into thinking the architecture uses a bias vector instead of a learned linear map (like a 1×1 convolution). The intended meaning is likely that this local linear component helps handle non-periodic boundary conditions, but equating it to a bias is imprecise.

Third, while the paper demonstrates superior accuracy and speed, a rigorous error analysis (e.g., convergence guarantees with respect to discretization parameters) is lacking, which would bolster the theoretical foundation. Additionally, the experimental sections (not fully provided here) should carefully control for confounding factors such as training data distribution and hyperparameter sensitivity. Overall, the work is innovative and well-written, but addressing these issues would improve its depth and generalizability.

comments.title: Potential confusion between 'Neural-FEM' and 'Neural-FEM' terminology
comments.quote: The second approach directly parameterizes the solution function as a neural network (E & Yu, 2018; Raissi et al., 2019; Bar & Sochen, 2019; Smith et al., 2020; Pan & Duraisamy, 2020). This approach is designed to model one specific instance of the PDE, not the solution operator. It is mesh-independent and accurate, but for any given new instance of the functional parameter/coefficient, it requires training a new neural network. The approach closely resembles classical methods such as finite elements, replacing the linear span of a finite set of local basis functions with the space of neural networks.
comments.explanation: The term 'Neural-FEM' is introduced in the overview section. The authors seem to use it as a label for methods that directly parameterize the solution function as a neural network. However, the cited references (E & Yu, 2018; Raissi et al., 2019) are typically associated with Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs), not with finite element concepts per se. The parallel to finite elements (replacing local basis functions with neural networks) is somewhat loose. While the authors correctly note that such methods require retraining for each new parameter instance, calling them 'Neural-FEM' might mislead readers into thinking they specifically build on Galerkin finite element structure. The name is not standard in the literature, and the description might cause confusion. However, since the authors explicitly explain what they mean, this is a minor framing concern rather than a real error.

comments.title: Missing justification for truncating Fourier modes and choice of k_max
comments.quote: We pick a finite-dimensional parameterization by truncating the Fourier series at a maximal number of modes k_max = |Z_{k_max}| = |{k ∈ Z^d : |k_j| ≤ k_{max,j}, for j = 1,...,d}|. We thus parameterize R_φ directly as complex-valued (k_max × d_v × d_v)-tensor... We note that the set Z_{k_max} is not the canonical choice for the low frequency modes of v_t. Indeed, the low frequency modes are usually defined by placing an upper-bound on the ℓ1-norm of k ∈ Z^d. We choose Z_{k_max} as above since it allows for an efficient implementation.
comments.explanation: The authors acknowledge that the truncation using a component-wise bound (|k_j| ≤ k_{max,j}) is non-canonical compared to an ℓ1-ball truncation, but claim it allows efficient implementation. They do not justify why this specific truncation (which retains 'corner' high-frequency modes in certain directions while discarding low-frequency modes along diagonals) does not compromise accuracy or lead to aliasing issues. Given that the Fourier layer performs a truncated convolution, the choice of truncation pattern directly affects which frequency components are preserved. The paper provides no analysis or experimental comparison to justify this choice. While the results are state-of-the-art, the lack of justification for an important design decision is a methodological gap.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of k_max and potential conflating of continuous and discrete modes
comments.quote: For frequency mode k ∈ D, we have (Fv_t)(k) ∈ C^{d_v} and R_φ(k) ∈ C^{d_v×d_v}. Notice that since we assume κ is periodic, it admits a Fourier series expansion, so we may work with the discrete modes k ∈ Z^d. We pick a finite-dimensional parameterization by truncating the Fourier series at a maximal number of modes k_max = |Z_{k_max}| = |{k ∈ Z^d : |k_j| ≤ k_{max,j}, for j = 1,...,d}|.
comments.explanation: There is a notation inconsistency between the continuous and discrete cases. The authors write 'k ∈ D' (where D is the physical domain) for the frequency mode in the continuous Fourier transform, but then switch to 'k ∈ Z^d' when assuming periodicity. This is confusing because in standard Fourier analysis, the frequency variable for a function on a bounded domain D is typically in the continuous dual space (R^d), not in D itself. Later, when discussing the discrete FFT case, they use k ∈ Z_{s_1} × ... × Z_{s_d}. The notation is overloaded: k represents a continuous frequency, a discrete Fourier mode index, and an FFT bin index. This could mislead a careful reader into conflating these distinct mathematical objects. While an experienced reader can infer the intended meaning from context, the passage should clarify the domain of k in each case.

comments.title: Questionable claim about 'first ML-based method to successfully model turbulent flows with zero-shot super-resolution'
comments.quote: The Fourier neural operator is the first ML-based method to successfully model turbulent flows with zero-shot super-resolution.
comments.explanation: This claim appears in the abstract and is also alluded to in the contributions list. The paper provides experimental evidence for zero-shot super-resolution on Navier-Stokes with Reynolds number 10000. However, calling this 'the first ML-based method' to achieve this is a strong historical claim that the paper does not substantiate with a thorough literature review. Prior work (e.g., super-resolution techniques for turbulent flows using GANs or physics-informed upscaling, or other neural operator architectures) might have achieved similar capabilities, even if not framed exactly the same way. Without a comprehensive comparison, this claim may overstate the novelty. Additionally, the Reynolds number 10000 turbulence regime is challenging, but the paper does not quantify what 'successfully model' means in terms of statistical metrics (e.g., energy spectra, correlation functions) versus visual similarity. The claim should be softened or accompanied by explicit comparisons to relevant prior work.

comments.title: Potential inconsistency in the description of the kernel integral operator's input dependence
comments.quote: If we remove the dependence on the function a and impose κ_φ(x,y) = κ_φ(x-y), we obtain that (3) is a convolution operator...
comments.explanation: In Definition 2, the kernel κ_φ is defined to have inputs (x, y, a(x), a(y)), i.e., it depends on the input function a at the evaluation points. The authors then say 'if we remove the dependence on the function a and impose κ_φ(x,y) = κ_φ(x-y)...' This correctly describes the specialization to a translation-invariant kernel. However, in the Fourier neural operator definition (Definition 3), they set R_φ to be the Fourier transform of a periodic function κ: D̅ → R^{d_v×d_v}, which is not explicitly stated to depend on a. In the 'Parameterizations of R' subsection, they mention an option to have R depend on (Fa), but the default (and used in experiments) is independent of a. This leap from the general form (dependent on a) to the Fourier-specific form (independent of a) is glossed over. The reader might wonder whether the Fourier neural operator loses the ability to handle non-translation-invariant kernels or input-dependent kernels that the original neural operator framework allowed. While the authors note this limitation and experiment with alternatives, the justification for why the translation-invariant (a-independent) version works well for PDE problems is missing. This is not a critical error but a missing discussion point.
